observations.managementAction: No action was noted on the part of Management to address the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: The Municipality has appointed a new OIC–MGSO, who is currently in the process of reviewing and familiarizing herself with the audit recommendation. Additional time is required to ensure a thorough and careful assessment. In the meantime, the Municipality is undertaking an ongoing reconciliation of records related to its land account.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year's recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the OIC – Municipal General Services Officer to consider availing the one-time cleansing of the PPE account, as provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 to address the proper disposition of non-existing or missing PPE items.
observations.managementAction: No action was noted on the part of Management to address the audit recommendation. However, upon reconciliation, a revised RPCPPE for CY 2024 was subsequently submitted.
observations.reason: The one-time cleansing of the PPE account, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, has yet to be undertaken by the Municipality due to the absence of a full-fledged Municipal General Services Officer. As a result, a variance amounting to P1,899,722.00 was still noted between the submitted CY 2024 RPCPPE and the records maintained by the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the OIC – Municipal General Services Officer appraise the donated MVs and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the appraisal report. After which, the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) shall recognize the donated MV in the books of account by preparing the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) and submit the same to this Office for verification.

Furthermore, we recommended that the OIC – Municipal General Services Officer issue a Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) to the office using or person in custody of the donated MVs to establish the accountability thereof.
observations.managementAction: No action was noted on the part of Management to address the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: As of CY 2024, an Appraisal Committee has not yet been established through the issuance of an EO. Consequently, the value of the donated MVs remains undetermined; hence, these assets have not yet been recorded in the books of accounts. A PAR could not be issued due to the absence of records maintained by the MGSO. Furthermore, the newly appointed OIC–MGSO was not aware of the said audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC – Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to cause the immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in Section 79 PD No. 1445 and COA- Department of Budget and Management (DBM) Joint Circular (JC) No. 2024-1 to prevent further deterioration and potentially generate additional income for the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: No action was noted on the part of Management to address the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Due to the absence of a full-fledged MGSO, the audit recommendation was not implemented. However, the newly appointed OIC–MGSO has expressed plans to implement the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts, which includes the disposal of the subject PPE items.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Make inquiries with the concerned Source Agency (SA) regarding the disposition of the non-moving unutilized balance totaling P100,471.34 and subsequently adopt the same; and
b. Remit the amount of P57,644.72 to the Bureau of Fire Protection (BFP) and ensure that all collections of the Fire Safety Inspection Fee (FSIF), supported by the Report on Collections and Deposits (RCD) and of records under the Due to NGAs - FSIF / Inspection Fee, are remitted within the prescribed period to avoid accumulation of unremitted balances in the SL.
observations.managementAction: No action was noted on the part of Management to address the audit recommendation. The amount of P57,644.72 was remitted in CY 2024. However, a balance of P42,307.12, collected by the Municipality during CY 2024, remained unremitted as of December 31, 2024.
observations.reason: The balance of the previously reported non-moving account under Due to NGAs remained unchanged. Consequently, the audit recommendation has yet to be implemented by the Management.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to:
a. Fill-up the vacant plantilla positions, giving priority to qualified JO workers; and
b. Coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer and thoroughly assess the organizational structure and staffing pattern of the Municipality, taking into consideration its service requirements and financial capability, subject to the minimum standards and guidelines prescribed by the Civil Service Commission.
observations.managementAction: As of September 25, 2024, the HRMO promoted eight JOs to permanent status and hired two additional permanent employees. However, as of December 31, 2024, there were still seven vacant plantilla positions, five of which were department head positions (Municipal Administrator, MPDO, MGSO, and Municipal Agriculturist). The HRMO coordinated with the Municipal Budget Officer and considered the financial capacity within the organizational structure and staffing pattern; however, 19 plantilla positions remained unfunded as of December 31, 2024.
observations.reason: The lack of qualified applicants for the vacant plantilla positions and the unfunded status of several positions indicated that the Municipality's financial capability was not fully considered in the formulation of its organizational structure and staffing pattern.

observations.managementAction:
In a letter reply dated May 8, 2024, the Municipal Accountant explained that the over-remittance of Pag-IBIG premiums and loan amortizations totaling P20,188.12 for the month of August, 2023 occurred due to the late submission of the Membership ID number of the former Municipal General Services Officer. As a result, the August remittance included amounts deducted in the previous month for that particular employee. The abnormal balance of P1,285.99 in the sub-ledger for MPL amortization in October 2023 was attributed to an over-refund to an employee; Official Receipt (OR) No. 2567598 dated May 8, 2024, representing the refunded amount, was submitted.

The MAO and HRMO conducted a reconciliation of records related to contributions for unregistered employees, which were subsequently remitted after their registration in CY 2024. The MAO is currently undertaking a reconciliation of the Due to PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG accounts. The MAO prioritizes corrections for accounts of retirees, officials nearing the end of their terms, and certain separated or resigned employees.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2024, 22 and 21 employees remained unregistered with PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG, respectively. The MAO is continuing reconciliation and prioritizes correction for retirees and separated employees; due to the voluminous transactions and records requiring verification, some unremitted amounts remain pending settlement.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Bids and
Awards Committee (BAC) and the OIC–Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO)
to provide a written explanation for the delays in the submission of perfected
contracts, POs, and Notices of Delivery.

Henceforth, we further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC
Secretariat and the OIC–MGSO to:
a. Submit copies of contracts, POs, and their supporting documents to the Office
of the Auditor within five working days from execution/issuance, in compliance
with Sections 3.1.1 and 3.2.2 of COA Circular No. 2009-001;
b. Furnish the Office of the Auditor with copies of delivery documents within 24
hours after acceptance of goods and services, as required under Section 6.9 of
COA Circular No. 2009-002, to facilitate timely inspection and review; and
c. Ensure that POs contain complete item specifications, including brand names,
in accordance with Item B of COA Circular No. 96-010.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MSWDO to develop and
implement comprehensive guidelines on the eligibility criteria, amount, and frequency
of financial assistance, aligned with DSWD MC No. 15, Series of 2022 and DSWD MC
No. 06, Series of 2023, and formalized through a SB Resolution to ensure transparency,
accountability, and fairness in fund allocation.

Furthermore, we also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and MSWDO to enforce strict compliance with documentary requirements
by ensuring that all financial aid requests are supported with complete and verified
documents before approval and disbursement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that any increase in IRA is
promptly reflected in the 20 percent DF through a Supplemental Budget, in
accordance with Section 287 of RA No. 7160;
b. Direct the LDC and concerned municipal offices to ensure that PPAs proposed
under the 20 percent DF are well-planned, procurement-ready, and
implementation-ready, in accordance with Section 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC
No. 1; and
c. Direct the concerned offices, including the MPDO and Budget Office, to revisit and
reassess the unimplemented PPAs under the current and continuing appropriations
and formulate timely realignment or reprogramming strategies to ensure effective
fund utilization.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to submit the required LDRRMFIP or
LDRRMP and revised AIP, ensuring that both documents clearly present the PPAs
implemented, along with the supplemental budget ordinance, to ensure compliance
with Item 6.5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all transactions
charged against the unexpended LDRRMF balances are properly documented with
complete supporting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to:
a. Establish and maintain a GAD Database to serve as the basis for gender-
responsive planning, programming, and policy formulation, as required under
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; and
b. Ensure that all succeeding GAD ARs include the required attachments, namely: a
summary of the reported program or project, copies of reported policy issuances,
results of HGDG tests, and actions taken by the LGU on COA audit findings and
recommendations. These attachments should be submitted within five working
days from the end of January of the ensuing year, in accordance with Section V of
COA Circular No. 2016-01, to facilitate the thorough evaluation and assessment of
GAD PPAs undertaken by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately reconcile the withheld and remitted
contributions and loan amortizations with the respective government institutions
and take corrective actions to address discrepancies.
b. Direct the Human Resource and Management Officer (HRMO) to:
   i. Coordinate with GSIS, PhilHealth, and Pag-IBIG to validate unregistered employees
   and facilitate their immediate registration, including retroactive documentation and
   settlement, where applicable;
   ii. Institutionalize a standard pre-employment procedure requiring the registration of
   newly hired employees with all mandatory government benefit agencies before the
   assumption of duties; and
   iii. Install and implement internal control mechanisms, such as periodic reconciliation
   of remittance records, validation of employee registration status, and regular
   coordination with the aforementioned agencies to prevent recurrence of similar
   issues.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to evaluate the necessity of the following:
a. Renewal of the appointment or updating of the designations of the DOs; 
b. Designating additional DOs to eliminate the granting of multiple CAs to a single DO; and 
c. Increasing the amount of bond due to the increase in the accountability of the DO.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Deny requests for additional CA if the DO has an existing unliquidated CA;
b. Install, implement and monitor a sound system of internal control in granting and subsequent liquidation of CAs; and
c. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002, and Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of CA.
observations.managementAction:
a. In a letter dated April 19, 2024, from the Municipal Treasurer, the Audit Team was informed that Ms. Joal C. Guan had served as the designated DO since assuming the position of Cashier II in 1995. Her designation ended in January 2023; hence, renewal of her appointment would not be necessary.
b. The Municipal Treasurer, with the concurrence of the Local Chief Executive, appointed Ms. Theresa L. Autencio as an additional DO through Office Order No. 2024-01 dated April 30, 2024.
c. The fidelity bond of Ms. Irene A. Tamondong is due for renewal in July 2024. Upon renewal, the bond is expected to be increased to adequately cover her total accountability. However, as indicated in the Confirmation Letter from the Bureau of the Treasury under Transmittal Letter No. 0434-2024-07-01913 dated July 10, 2024, the approved bond amount for the DO was not increased and remained at P750,000.

Regarding the Municipal Accountant:
a. No action was noted on the part of Management to deny requests for additional CA if the DO has an existing unliquidated CA. Multiple cash advances were still granted to DOs despite the non-liquidation of previous advances, due to the nature of the cash advances assigned to each DO and the urgent necessity of these advances.
b. As an additional internal control measure, DOs were assigned based on the nature of the cash advance (e.g., Payroll Fund, Operating Expenses, etc.). The Municipal Treasurer, with the concurrence of the Local Chief Executive, appointed Ms. Theresa L. Autencio as an additional DO through Office Order No. 2024-01 dated April 30, 2024. The Office also indicated that Ms. Irene A. Tamondong's bond is due for renewal and expected to be increased upon renewal.
c. The provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002, and Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 were observed. However, due to manpower constraints and the urgency of some cash advances, assigning additional DOs to prevent the granting of multiple cash advances to a DO with an outstanding/unliquidated CA was not feasible in some instances.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer appointed an additional DO and measures were instituted to assign DOs based on the nature of cash advances. However, the approved bond amount for the DO was not increased and remained at P750,000. Due to the nature of the cash advances and urgent necessity, multiple advances were still granted in some cases; hence, the audit recommendation related to denying additional advances when unliquidated balances exist remains partially constrained by operational needs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the BAC and the BAC Secretariat to:
a. Ensure that the APPs for CY 2024 and in the succeeding years are under the requirements under GPPB Circular No. 07-2015;
b. Prepare and submit the approved PMR within 14 calendar days after the end of each semester following the format prescribed by the GPPB, copy furnish this Office for review and audit; and
c. Prospectively, strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184, its 2016 Revised IRR, and GPPB Circular No. 07-2015.
observations.managementAction:
a. APPs for CY 2024 complied with the requirements set forth in GPPB Circular No. 07-2015.
b. The approved PMRs were consistently submitted within 14 calendar days after the end of each semester, using the format prescribed by the GPPB, starting from the second semester of CY 2024, upon receipt of the audit recommendation by Management.
c. The provisions of RA No. 9184, its 2016 Revised IRR, and GPPB Circular No. 07-2015 were strictly adhered to to ensure compliance in the procurement activities.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal General Services Officer, in coordination with the concerned Department Heads, to:
a. Provide a written explanation for the absence of “For Official Use Only” marking on 17 MVs, the lack of Municipal logo/seal on nine MVs, and the presence of a removable magnetic seal on one MV; 
b. Permanently mark the Municipality MVs with “For Official Use Only”, affix Municipal Logo/seal to nine MVs, and install temporary conduction plates for the three motorcycles and one tricycle; and
c. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions set forth under COA Circular No. 75-6 dated November 7, 1975.
observations.managementAction: No explanation was provided by the Management on the absence of the required markings for the 17 MVs. Nonetheless, the OIC–MGSO procured stickers with “For Official Use Only” and Municipal Logo/Seal and installed the same to the MVs. The provisions of COA Circular No. 75-6 were strictly implemented thereafter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the OIC – Municipal Planning and Development Officer revisit the work and financial plan and evaluate the property for its proper disposition/decision and allocate the necessary funds for the repair and/or completion of the Multi-Purpose Building.
observations.managementAction: Submitted copies of the proposed plan, designs and estimated project cost for the rehabilitation of the San Isidro Hills Eco-Tourism Park. Furthermore, the Sangguniang Bayan issued Resolution No. 005-2024 dated January 16, 2024, authorizing the Municipal Mayor to negotiate with the Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) or the Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP). However, the proposed loan from an Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) did not materialize. The loan was intended to finance 10 developmental projects amounting to P184,500,000.00, including the rehabilitation of the San Isidro Hills.
observations.reason: The proposed loan from an AGDB did not materialize. The planned rehabilitation proposals and documents were prepared and submitted, but financing through the proposed loan was not consummated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator as its secretariat, and the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the re-classification of the charging of the procurement of the disaster equipment to the 5 percent LDRRMF, and correspondingly, reinstate the budget for 20 percent DF.
observations.managementAction: In a letter reply dated May 20, 2024 of the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer, it was explained that the procurement of the specialized drone was initially charged against the 20 percent DF with the understanding that its use for disaster risk reduction and management aligns with the broader objectives of municipal development and safety. Henceforth, beginning CY 2024, all appropriations and utilization of the 20 percent DF will be in strict compliance with the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 01.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Disaster Risk and Response Officer to:
a. Immediately submit a revised LDRRMFIP which includes the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;
b. Stop the charging of ineligible expenses under the Municipality’s five percent LDRRMF; otherwise, the disbursements/transactions shall be disallowed in audit; and
c. Henceforth, strictly adhere to governing rules and regulations on budgeting, utilization, accounting and reporting of the LDRRMF set forth by RA No. 10121, NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1, and COA Circular No. 2012-002.

In addition, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to re-classify the charging of the procurement of eight units of CCTVs costing P94,800.00 to the Municipality’s General Fund and correspondingly, reinstate the budget for LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction:
The OIC – MDRRMO submitted Resolution No. 02A-2023, authorizing and approving the revised LDRRMFIP for the unexpended balances from previous years’ appropriations (CY 2022), as well as the LDRRMF continuing appropriation for 2022, and furnished a copy to the Audit Team on May 8, 2024. The procurement amounting to P94,800.00 was reinstated in the LDRRMF budget through Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) Nos. 100-2024-12-218D and 300-2024-12-017C during CY 2024.

However, the Audit Team noted that as of this writing, P392,370.00 from prior years’ unexpended balance of the LDRRMF, transferred to the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF (2-04-01-020) account, was not supported by the required LDRRMFIP or LDRRMP, revised AIP, and Supplemental Budget approved by the Sangguniang Bayan through an Ordinance.
observations.reason: Although the procurement of CCTV amounting to P94,800.00 was reclassified and reinstated, the Audit Team is still awaiting the complete submission of the LDRRMFIP or LDRRMP, the revised AIP, and the corresponding supplemental budget ordinance that clearly reflect the PPAs implemented. The absence of these required supporting documents casts doubt on the validity of P392,370.00 disbursements from prior years’ unexpended balances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended, henceforth, that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to establish a RC for GFPS to ensure that GAD-related transactions are properly monitored and accurately accounted for.
observations.managementAction: The OIC – Municipal Budget Officer established the RC for the GFPS to ensure that GAD-related transactions are properly monitored and accounted for.

A written explanation on the occurrence of over- and/or under-remittance of the taxes withheld from the employees’ compensation and payments to suppliers and GSIS premiums and loan amortizations was provided. Upon initial verification by the MAO, it was found that taxes withheld from an allowance for COVID workers, totaling P131,737.50, had already been remitted to the BIR. However, the withheld amount remained recorded in the Trust Fund (TF) books. The MAO promptly facilitated the transfer of this amount to rectify the discrepancy.

The Municipality acknowledged that discrepancies persist between amounts withheld and actual remittances to government agencies. The MAO is actively engaged in ongoing efforts to address these issues in collaboration with the GSIS. Designated HR personnel responsible for preparing payroll and remittance schedules maintain regular coordination with the MAO focal person to review and identify monthly variances. Several causes were identified, including delays in registration, system updates at GSIS for salary changes, and adjustments during reconciliation efforts. The MAO indicated that they will continue reconciliation and corrective actions.
observations.reason: The MAO provided explanations and initiated reconciliations with concerned agencies. However, discrepancies persist and require ongoing reconciliation efforts, particularly for retirees, officials nearing end of terms, and separated employees. The process is voluminous and prioritized for specific groups, hence some variances remain pending correction.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services Officer, spearheaded by the Municipal General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant, to undertake all the procedures in the conduct of one-time cleansing of the PPE account balances prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006.
Likewise, the Municipal General Services Officer to facilitate the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties totaling P8,123,546.41 thru the submission of a request for disposal to the Office of the Auditor supported with the duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), Appraisal Report and other required documents for evaluation and inspection in accordance with the rules and procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 92-386.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the accounting standards governing the recognition criteria for PPE as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2022-004 and IPSAS 17.
observations.managementAction:
A copy of Executive Order No. 5-2022, creating the Municipal Inventory Committee of the implementation of COA Circular No. 2020-006 executed by the Municipal Mayor on August 2, 2022, together with the proposed Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) was submitted to the Office of the Auditor on September 14, 2022.
Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 2, page nos. 35-37, Part II of this Report.
On April 4, 2023, the Municipal Accountant furnished the Audit Team with the approved PIP, which is scheduled to commence on May 1, 2023. As outlined in the plan, the one-time cleansing is to be completed no later than July 31, 2023.
Regarding the unreconciled items, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer have decided to defer the drawing up of the adjusting journal entries until the completion of the one-time cleansing. This decision aims to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the identified reconciling items.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer to facilitate the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties totaling P8,123,546.41 thru the submission of a request for disposal to the Office of the Auditor supported with the duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), Appraisal Report and other required documents for evaluation and inspection in accordance with the rules and procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 92-386.
The disposal of the unserviceable properties will be incorporated into the scheduled one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts, ensuring the proper disposition of all the Municipality's unserviceable properties.
observations.reason: Due to absence of a full-fledged Municipal General Services Officer. As a result, the disposal of the unserviceable properties was not facilitated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the implementation of the project by providing a timeline of the implementation of the project by providing the revised plans, drawings and the proposed detailed cost estimates. Furthermore, that the Municipal Mayor as the Head of the Procuring Entity (HoPE) direct the Chairperson of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that no bidding and award of contracts for infrastructure projects shall be made unless the detailed engineering investigations, surveys and designs for the project have been sufficiently carried out and duly approved, pursuant to Section 17.6, Rule VI of the R-IRR of RA No. 9184. Also, that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer seek legal advice on the available remedies against the defaulting contractor.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Engineer confirmed that, after the inspection conducted by the Audit Team, the noted structural defects were addressed by the contractor. Additionally, the proposed plan for the slaughterhouse did not materialize due to unforeseen indispensable projects within the Municipality. Nonetheless, the Municipality is currently in the process of applying for a loan agreement with an Accredited Government Depository Bank (AGDB) to finance the ongoing projects for the slaughterhouse.
No legal action was taken against the contractor who defaulted.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Head of the Procuring Entity (HoPE) direct the Chairperson of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that no bidding and award of contracts for infrastructure projects shall be made unless the detailed engineering investigations, surveys and designs for the project have been sufficiently carried out and duly approved pursuant to Section 17.6, Rule VI of the R-IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.reason: Terminating the current contract with the contractor, as outlined in Annex "I" of the R-IRR of RA No. 9184, would serve as a remedy to absolve both the Municipality and the contractor of their respective accountabilities. However, the audit recommendation remained unimplemented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the BAC and the BAC
Secretariat to:

a. Ensure that the APPs for CY 2024 and in the succeeding years are under the requirements
under GPPB Circular No. 07-2015;

b. Prepare and submit the approved PMR within 14 calendar days after the end of each
semester following the format prescribed by the GPPB, copy furnish this Office for review
and audit; and

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management
Officer to:

a. Fill-up the vacant plantilla positions, giving priority to qualified JO workers; and

b. Coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer and thoroughly assess the organizational
structure and staffing pattern of the Municipality, taking into consideration its service
requirements and financial capability, subject to the minimum standards and guidelines
prescribed by the Civil Service Commission.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal General Services
Officer, in coordination with the concerned Department Heads, to:

a. Provide a written explanation for the absence of “For Official Use Only” markings on 17
Motor Vehicles (MVs), the lack of Municipal logo/seal on nine MVs did, and the presence
of a removable magnetic seal on one MV;
