Logo/seal to nine MVs, and install temporary conduction plates for the three motorcycles
and one tricycle; and

c. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions set forth under COA Circular No. 75-6 dated
November 7, 1975.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the OIC – Municipal Planning and
Development Officer revisit the work and financial plan and provide a written
explanation/justification as to why until to date, the building remains unutilized/idle to date.

Furthermore, we recommended the Municipal Mayor, together with the OIC – Municipal
Planning and Development Officer, Municipal Engineer, and OIC – Municipal Budget Officer,
evaluate the property for its proper disposition/decision and allocate the necessary funds for
the repair and/or completion of the Multi-Purpose Building.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Development Council, the
Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator as its secretariat, and the OIC – Municipal
Budget Office to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the re-classification of the
charging of the procurement of the disaster equipment to the 5 percent Local Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF), and correspondingly, reinstate the budget for
20 percent DF.

Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 01 on the
appropriation and utilization of the 20 percent DF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Disaster Risk and
Response Officer (MDRRMO) to:

a. Immediately submit a revised LDRRMFIP which includes the list of projects and
activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;

b. Stop the charging of ineligible expenses under the Municipality’s 5 percent LDRRMF;
otherwise, the disbursements/transactions shall be disallowed in audit

c. Henceforth, strictly adhere to governing rules and regulations on budgeting,
utilization, accounting and reporting of the LDRRMF set forth by RA No. 10121,
NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1, and COA Circular No. 2012-002.

In addition, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to re-classify the charging of the procurement of eight units of
CCTVs costing P94,800.00 to the Municipality’s General Fund and correspondingly, reinstate
the budget for LDRRMF.

We recommended, henceforth, that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC – Municipal Budget
Officer and Municipal Accountant to establish a RC for GFPS to ensure that GAD-related
transactions are properly monitored and accounted for.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Provide a written explanation on the occurrence of over and/or under remittance of the
taxes withheld from the employees’ compensation and payments to suppliers and the
employees’ GSIS premiums and loan amortizations;

b. Analyze the Subsidiary Ledger (SL) accounts of the Due to BIR (2-02-01-010) account
under the General Fund (GF) and Special Education Fund (SEF) and Due to GSIS (2-02-
01-020), specifically focusing on the payments of employees’ loan amortization for GSIS
Financial Assistance Loan (GFAL), Multi-Purpose Loan (MPL) and Computer Loan, which
exhibit abnormal debit balances of P19,303.45, P19.26, P34,860.82, P17,031.73 and
P10,986.01, respectively, after the posting of the remittances for the month of December,
2023. Draw the corresponding adjusting Journal Entry Voucher (JEV), if warranted, and
furnish the Audit Team for verification; and

c. Verify the unremitted withholding taxes and GSIS premiums and/or loan amortizations,
and immediately remit or effect the necessary adjustment, if warranted; and

d. Henceforth, strictly monitor the withholding of taxes and GSIS premiums and/or loan
amortizations to avoid accumulation of unremitted balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Human Resource Management
Officer to immediately register of the five employees with Pag-IBIG.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Provide a written explanation on the occurrence of: (a) over-remittance of employees Pag-
IBIG premiums and loan amortizations totaling P20,188.12 for the month of August, 2023
and (b) abnormal balance of P1,285.99 in the sub-ledger for the Multi-Purpose Loan (MPL)
amortization during the month of October, 2023;

b. Coordinate with the Human Resource and Management Officer and reconcile the amount
pertaining to the contributions withheld for employees not registered with PhilHealth and
Pag-IBIG against identified under-remittance each month and remit the same if necessary;

c. Verify the unremitted prior years PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG premiums totaling P226,116.23
and P200,633.03, respectively, and immediately remit or effect the necessary adjustment,
if warranted;

d. Henceforth, strictly monitor the monthly withholding and remittance of PhilHealth and
Pag-IBIG premiums and/or loan amortizations to avoid accumulation of unremitted
balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis in setting up the RPT/SET Receivable at the beginning of the year;
b. Municipal Accountant to provide SLs containing the details or breakdown of the balances of the RPT and SET Receivables; and
c. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to analyze and investigate the cause/s of the deficiency amounting to P1,273,361.03 between the balance of the RPT Receivables and the Certified List of Delinquent Taxes prepared by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office as of year-end and make the necessary adjustments in their respective records, if warranted.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) utilized the assessment roll as the basis for establishing the RPT/SET Receivable at the beginning of the year; the Municipal Accountant countered that the Municipality already employs the Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR), a system utilized by both the MAO and MTO. Therefore, maintaining an additional Subsidiary Ledger for RPT Receivables might appear redundant and impractical.
The balances of the RPT Receivables recorded in the books of accounts were reconciled with the Certified List of Delinquent Taxes prepared by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office as of year-end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances; otherwise, cause the withholding of the salaries of the Accountable Officers pursuant to Section 5.1.3 and 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor along with the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant re-evaluate the necessity of maintaining a PCF; and if found unnecessary, cause its immediate liquidation.
observations.managementAction:
The cash advance granted for the official travel amounting P22,800.00 was refunded on January 26, 2023, with Official Receipt Number 992882. Meanwhile, the cash advance granted for the pass way of Municipality vehicles used for dumping of garbage collection, amounting P60,000.00, was liquidated.
Likewise, the Municipal General Services Officer at that time, who also concurrently served as the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer, chose to continue maintaining a petty cash fund for passageway and dumping expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate settlement of the accountabilities of the former Municipal Treasurer by initiating immediate legal actions pursuant to COA Circular No. 90-329.
observations.managementAction: On November 23, 2023, the 5th Division of the Sandiganbayan rendered its decision and found Mr. Elizalde G. Gabaleño, the Former Municipal Treasurer of this Municipality, guilty beyond reasonable doubt on his case of malversation of public funds. His undeposited collection totaled P339,523.44, recorded under the Other Receivables account, which remained outstanding for over 15 years. Consequently, he is ordered to pay a fine of P483,529.31, with a legal interest of 6 percent per annum, reckoned from the finality of this decision until satisfaction.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Assessor to:
a. Update the SFMV and submit the same to the Provincial Assessor’s Office for review;
b. Closely coordinate with the Provincial Assessor’s Office to fast track the general revision of the real property assessment upon the submission of the updated SFMVs; and
c. Prospectively undertake general revision of real property assessment every three years as prescribed by Section 219 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Assessor informed the Audit Team that several actions were undertaken in preparation for the General Revision of the SFMVs. These included conducting a series of cluster meetings with other Local Government Units (LGUs) and the Office of the Provincial Assessor (OPA) in April 2016 to deliberate on proposed SFMVs. Subsequently, the OPA endorsed the proposed SFMVs to the Bureau of Local Government Finance (BLGF) – CALABARZON on May 17, 2017. In CY 2019, the OPA disseminated the findings of the BLGF evaluation and its recommendation to resubmit, along with the SFMVs for Agricultural Land and Buildings. Further, in CY 2022, consultation and coordination were conducted by the OPA to arrive at revised SFMVs for Buildings.
The Provincial Assessor’s Office has coordinated with the OPA, which has completed the proposed SFMVs of land for all Municipalities within Laguna Province. Additionally, the OPA is finalizing the Schedule of Base Unit Construction Cost (BUCC) in coordination with City Assessors of the province. Once completed, the schedule will be submitted to BLGF – CALABARZON for review and subsequently endorsed to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for ordinance approval and enactment.
The responsibility for conducting the general revision of Real Property Assessment for Municipalities and enacting a Provincial Ordinance, as mandated by RA No. 7160, is vested in the OPA. However, the Municipality is committed to carrying out the general revision of real property assessment every three years, as prescribed by Section 219 of RA No. 7160.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to, henceforth, submit copies of Contracts, POs, and its supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five working days after its perfection/issuance in compliance with Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001. Likewise, furnish the Office of the Auditor with a copy of delivery documents within 24 hours after acceptance of deliveries of goods and services as required under Section 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002 to facilitate the timely conduct of inspection of the deliveries.
observations.managementAction:
Copies of contracts, POs, and its supporting documents were submitted to the Office of the Auditor within the regulatory period set under COA Circular No. 2009-001.
Copies of the delivery documents were furnished to the Office of the Auditor within 24 hours after acceptance of deliveries of goods and services as required under Section 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to strictly adhere with Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 on the submission of GAD Accomplishment Report for the timely review and evaluation of the implementation of the proposed GAD programs, projects and activities.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Accomplishment Report was submitted to the Office of the Auditor within the deadline set under COA Circular No. 2014-001.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer to reconcile the discrepancies noted between the RPCPPE and accounting records and effect the adjustments to arrive at reconciled balances, and to maintain updated PCs and PPELCs as basis for reconciliation and validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal Engineer to furnish the Accounting Office with copies
of reports on completed and on-going projects, including the respective
percentage of completion, for efficient monitoring of the PPAs to be included
in the Construction in Progress account;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to reconcile
their records and determine the correct balances of the CIP accounts; and
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary adjusting entry upon
reconciliation to correct any understatement or overstatement in the
Construction in Progress account of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
request details of the Due from LGU and Due to LGU accounts from the
Provincial Accountant and reconcile balances for the reciprocal accounts
appearing in the books of the Municipality.

We also recommended that the necessary adjustments be made upon
reconciliation to correct the discrepancy to arrive at an accurate and correct
balance of the account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant to
initiate action in cleaning the dormant receivables in the books of account in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005. Also, direct the Municipal
Accountant and other official concerned to:
a. Comply with COA Circular No. 2016-005 to accurately reflect reliable amount
of receivables in the Financial Statements;
b. Consider requesting for authority to write-off dormant receivables subject to
the conditions set forth by the above circular and submit to the Auditor for
review and evaluation;
c. Set up Allowance for Impairment for recognition of possible losses and effect
the adjustment in the amount of asset reflected in the Financial Statements
for fair presentation; and
d. Still continue to exert effort for its collectability when circumstances would
warrant.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Create an Inventory and Disposal Committee to undertake the disposal
procedures of unserviceable property in compliance with Section 79 of PD No.
1445; and
b. Instruct the General Service Officer to coordinate with the Municipal
Accountant to effect the necessary adjustment upon disposal of property as
provided by IPSAS No. 17.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor comply with the provision of COA
Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and EO No. 431 dated May 30, 2005
and direct the Municipal Accountant to transfer/return the unutilized balances of
Due to NGAs to enable the Source Agencies to utilize these funds for other
priority programs and projects of the Agency and to clear the unliquidated Due to
NGAs in the books.

Also, direct the concerned officials for immediate implementation and utilization
of the trust fund received so as not to deprive the intended beneficiaries of the
benefits to be derived therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to:
a. Institute proper planning in the preparation of detailed engineering and
program of works, before the awarding of contract and implementation of
infrastructure projects to avoid revision that often cause extension and
suspension which resulted in delayed completion;
b. Require the contractor to immediately correct the technical defects noted in
the inspection otherwise, inform them of the consequences like inclusion in
the Blacklisted contractors in case of breach of contract such as non-
completion or abandonment of the project.
c. Evaluate the work accomplished by the contractor and impose liquidated
damages at least one-tenth of one per cent (0.1%) of the cost of the
unperformed portion for every day of delay; and
d. Strictly monitor the implementation of the project in order to avoid delay that
will deprive the Municipality and the constituents of the timely receipt of
benefits that may be derived from the project.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services
Officer and the Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) Secretariat and submit copies of Contracts, Purchase Orders
and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five working
days after its perfection/issuance, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

Likewise, we reiterated our audit recommendation that the Auditor be furnished
with copies of delivery documents as required under COA Circular No. 2009-002
to facilitate the timely conduct of inspection of the deliveries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services
Officer to prepare the list of insurable properties and facilitate the
filing/application for the necessary insurance coverage of all these assets with the
GSIS under the General Insurance Fund as required under RA No. 656, as
amended and COA Circular No. 92-390 dated November 17, 1992.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Development Council
to continue its commitment to maximize the implementation of the development
projects under the 20 per cent Development Fund to attain the maximum benefits
that can be derived by the constituents from those PPAs.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the immediate utilization
and implementation of the remaining unimplemented projects in order to fully
achieve the 100 per cent realization and completion of development projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all concerned offices to
optimize the utilization of the LDRRM Fund to strengthen its capacity to mitigate
disaster risk in order to ensure and maintain the maximum implementation of
disaster mitigation and preparedness programs/projects/activities (PPAs) under
the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point Designate
to monitor the strict implementation of plans, programs and activities included in
the GPB and fully utilize the GAD Budget to achieve its goal of promoting gender-
responsive governance, protecting and fulfilling women’s human rights and
promoting women’s empowerment.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point
Designate and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to consider the
current COVID-19 pandemic situation in the preparation of budgeted plans,
programs and activities under GAD Fund to ensure that PPAs will be fully
implemented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
a. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer to reconcile the discrepancies noted between the RPCPPE and the Accounting records and effect the adjustments to arrive at reconciled balances, and to maintain updated PEC and PPELCs as basis for reconciliation and validation;
b. The Municipal Accountant to reclassify the PPE totaling P1,506,571.43 to each appropriate PPE accounts according to its classification, specification and nature; and
c. The Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop the small value property items from the books by debiting Prior Years’ Adjustment of P481,550.52 and Accumulated Depreciation of P15,000.00 and by crediting the respective PPE accounts of P1,368,528.13.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting and General Services Offices exerted efforts to reconcile records and locate the PPE in both inventory and accounting records. Two Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs), for the General Fund and Special Education Fund (SEF), dated June 21, 2021 were drawn to recognize the reclassification of the PPE items into their respective proper accounts. Likewise, two JEVs dated June 21, 2021 were also drawn to drop semi-expendable items that do not meet the capitalization threshold.
observations.reason: Some PPE items procured and recorded prior to CY 1995 were presented as lump sum in the inventory and accounting records due to the inability to locate these items.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer to reconcile the discrepancies noted between the RPCPPE and the Accounting records and effect the adjustments to arrive at reconciled balances and to maintain updated PEC and PPELCs as basis for reconciliation and validation; and
b. henceforth, strictly observe the proper classification of accounts as stated under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office committed to strictly monitor the recording of the accounts into the proper classifications in compliance with the cited COA Circular.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the documents needed for recording and recognition of the exchange transactions involving the award and sale of lots to employees under the CSAP of the Municipality. Also draw Journal Entry Voucher to set up the receivables, adjust the Land account and recognize the revenue for the partial payment received/collected, to present fairly the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: A JEV dated June 1, 2021 was drawn and the Land receivables were set up, land account adjusted, and income from prior years recognized.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances between their records pertaining to Cash in Bank- Local Currency Current Account and immediately effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct account’s balance.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting and Treasury Offices coordinated to assure that reported balance of the cash account in the Financial Statements reconciles with the balances in the cash book and the Subsidiary Ledgers (SLs).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to request details of the Due from LGU and Due to LGU accounts from the Provincial Accountant and reconcile balances for the reciprocal accounts appearing in the books of the Municipality. We also recommended that the necessary adjustment be made upon reconciliation to correct the discrepancy to arrive at an accurate and correct balance of the account in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the Provincial Accountant to identify the accounts in the books of the Provincial Government and the Municipality as reasons for the inability to fully implement the audit recommendation. Adjustments were made and recorded; remittances of both parties were also made.
observations.reason: The Accounting Office cited the basis and timing differences in recording reciprocal accounts in the books of the Provincial Government and the Municipality as reasons for the inability to fully implement the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to review all the accounting entries that affect both reciprocal accounts, determine the causes of discrepancy between their balances, and make the necessary adjustment to reconcile the balance of the reciprocal accounts to ensure reliability of the reflected balance in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The variance between the reciprocal accounts resulted from the payment of interest for the Municipality’s loan. The balances of both accounts were corrected and adjusted in January 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Vouchers to adjust the account in the General Fund and Trust Fund books and reflect the correct balance of Trust Liabilities – DRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has recognized the transfer of the unexpended balances of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE at year-end prior to the closing of the books for CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with other concerned officials to initiate action in cleaning the dormant receivables in the books of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 through the following: a. Exert more effort in enforcing the settlement of subject accounts including that of the unremitted and collections of former Assistant Municipal Treasurer; and b. Consider requesting for authority to write-off dormant receivables if collectability is established to be nil, subject to the conditions set forth by the above circular and submit to the Audit Team for review and evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The Management has not yet contacted the former Assistant Municipal Treasurer requesting to offset his terminal leave pay awaits clarifications based on rules set by government provisions on off-setting.
observations.reason: The Management was not able to maximize their effort to locate debtors due to the restrictions caused by COVID-19 pandemic. The inability to locate debtors prohibited the Management to complete the necessary documentation to request for write-off.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the immediate submission of the 18 DVs and their supporting documents. Henceforth, require submission of accounts within the period prescribed under Section 100 of PD No.1445 and COA Circular No. 2009-006. The remedies provided under Section 122 of PD No.1445 may be availed of in instances of unjustified failure to submit said accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office submitted the subject deficient documents to the Office of the Auditor. The Management ensured that processed and paid DVs are forwarded to the COA Office within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC Secretariat to comply and adhere to the rules and regulations set forth in RA No. 9184 or the Government Procurement Reform Act and to its Revised IRR as regards the submission of required documentation in the procurement of Infrastructure Projects so as to promote transparency, accountability, equity, efficiency, and economy in the procurement process.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office submitted copies of perfected POs and contracts to the Office of the Auditor in CY 2021. However, no reason was provided for the inability to implement the audit recommendation earlier.
observations.reason: Aside from the inability to submit copies of perfected contracts within the prescribed time, printed copies of posting to the PhilGEPS of the Notice of Award, Contract and Notice to Proceed were not provided. No reason was provided for the inability to implement the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant:
i. To record purchases of office supplies, accountable forms, drugs and medicines, medical, dental and laboratory supplies, food supplies, and other supplies as inventories and issue the same to the end-user. The Accounting Office to record recognized purchases and issuances of inventories, evidenced with Subsidiary Ledgers, and to close the inventories to the appropriate supplies expense accounts as applicable.
ii. To maintain the Subsidiary Ledgers (SLs) for the inventories and issuance of each class of supplies and materials.
iii. To record the issuances based on the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI).
b. Municipal General Services Officer:
i. To maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies; the stock cards to be furnished to the Accounting Office with copies.
ii. To prepare and submit monthly the RSMI to the Municipal Accountant for the recording of issuance documents; and
iii. To prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) by type of Inventory as of a given date.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office recorded purchases and submitted the subject deficient documents to the Office of the Audit to comply with the audit recommendation. The General Services Office recorded supplies inventory and continuously updated the stock cards. The GSO provided the Accounting Office with copies. The Accounting Office prepared Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRM Fund which was forwarded to the LDRRM Office and the Office of the Auditor. The Accounting Office maintained subsidiary ledgers and has been adjusting accounting records on a quarterly basis. However, individual SLs for each class of supplies and materials are still for updating. The GSO cited lack of manpower and lack of stockroom as reasons.
observations.reason: The readily available records were for the inventories categorized per class such as office supplies; individual SLs are still for updating. The unavailability of stockroom to house inventories prompted immediate distribution so full implementation of stock/card controls was not achieved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO to cause the disposal of property reported as serviceable and unserviceable in the IIRUP in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and Section 170 to 180 of the Manual on the Rules and Procedures on Management of Property for Local Government Units. Upon disposal, direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Voucher in order to derecognize these properties and adjust the overstatement of PPE account in the books as required under IPSAS No. 17.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer is in the process of assessing the list of properties to determine the current status of the PPE and to provide recommendation for mode of disposal which included donation, public bidding, sale through negotiation and condemnation. Management assured continuous procedures for disposal. The Municipal Accountant committed that necessary adjustments will be effected upon disposal.
observations.reason: Updating of records has not been completed at year-end; some unserviceable properties from CY 2003 and earlier years were not yet located.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services Officer and the Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the BAC Secretariat and submit copies of Contracts, Purchase Orders and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five working days after its perfection/issuance, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001. Likewise, furnish the Auditor with copies of delivery documents as required under COA Circular No. 2009-002 to facilitate the timely conduct of inspection of the deliveries.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office submitted copies of perfected POs and contracts to the Office of the Auditor in CY 2021. Copies of delivery documents were submitted to COA as supporting attachments to processed and paid DVs.
observations.reason: Most of the POs/contracts were submitted beyond the prescribed time while others were not submitted at all. Submission within 24 hours after acceptance of delivery was not strictly adhered to.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to release the barangays’ shares from RPTI within five days after the end of each quarter as required under Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: From 44 to 388 days in delay during CY 2020, this was reduced to 4 to 44 days in CY 2021. This was due to technical problems encountered in the Municipality’s accounting system during the updates to guidelines for the new GAM for LGUs which the system provider addressed.
observations.reason: Technical problems in the Municipality’s accounting system during updates to guidelines for the new GAM for LGUs caused delays.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursement vouchers are processed and paid with complete documentation to establish the propriety of expenses in compliance with Section 9.1.3.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and submit the lacking documents listed in the Appendices to comply with the documentary requirements for the specified transactions.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office submitted the subject deficient documents to the Office of the Auditor to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality provide specific budget equivalent to one percent (1%) of IRA for LCPC and cause the maximum utilization of the allocated fund to identified PPAs provided in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012, and prepare Local Development Plan and Local Investment Plan for Children and inform the Sangguniang Bayan on the PPAs that can be funded by the one percent IRA geared towards the achievement of objectives on the promotion of rights and welfare of the children in the community.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2021, the Municipal Government appropriated P1,942,902.88 for LCPC which is higher than the required P1,282,022.64. PPAs for the LCPC fund were specifically mentioned in Appropriation Ordinance No. 01-2021 (the Municipal Budget for CY 2021) deliberated and reviewed by the Sangguniang Bayan on January 6, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Development Council to continue its commitment to maximize the implementation of the development projects under the 20 percent Development Fund and enjoin the immediate utilization and implementation of the remaining unimplemented projects in order to fully achieve the 100 percent realization and completion of development projects.
observations.managementAction: The Management was able to utilize 94.52 per cent of the adjusted appropriation for the 20 percent Development Fund in CY 2021 and committed that implementation of the remaining allotted budget for development projects will be realized.
observations.reason: Two projects started in CY 2020 (Construction of Slaughter House, Rehabilitation/Construction of Multi-Purpose Building) were still not completed as of December 31, 2021. The delay for the first project was due to revision of plan and program of works.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from charging expenses to the 20 percent DF which do not partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
observations.managementAction: In compliance with the recommendation, the Management caused the immediate stoppage of the charging of wages of Job Order personnel to the 20 percent Development Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE at year-end to the Special Trust Fund books, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has recognized the transfer of the unexpended balances of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE at year-end prior to the closing of the books for CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO Property Officer to prepare the Inventory List of Procured/Donated Items for DRRM and submit the same to the Audit Team for monitoring in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-02.
observations.managementAction: Procured/Donated items relating to LDRRM were submitted to the Audit Team. A copy of the Inventory List of Procured/Donated items relating to LDRRM was submitted to the Audit Office.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require both the LDRR Officer and the Municipal Accountant to prepare the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRM Fund, and submit the same to the COA duly certified by the Municipal Accountant, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office prepared the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRM Fund which was forwarded to the LDRRM Office and the Office of the Auditor. The Audit Team was also furnished with a copy of the consolidated report covering January to December 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to execute Contract Agreement for the services of Waste disposal and comply with the documentary requirements of submitting contract before effecting payment. Also submit to the Auditor the perfected and approved contract for evaluation as provided in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 to ascertain the propriety of the transactions.
observations.managementAction: Per copy of Contract Agreement provided to the Office of the Auditor, the Municipality entered into a contract for dumping of municipal waste with Global Environmental Technologies and General Services (GEMTECH) Inc., on May 11, 2021 for a total contract price of P2,731,880.00. Copy of the perfected contract was submitted to COA Office upon request for audit of CY 2021 accounts.
observations.reason: Aside from the copy of the contract submitted, some supporting documents such as basis for estimate of the 6,520 m3 municipal waste, and quotation of GEMTECH Inc. were not provided; the submitted copy was not notarized. These deficiencies led to the inability of the Audit Team to verify the validity of the Contract fully earlier.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the MSWMB and the MENRO review and update the Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality every two years or as the need arises, and coordinate with stakeholders including barangay officials regarding proper waste disposal and promotion of household composting to reduce solid wastes.
observations.managementAction: The MENRO Designate coordinated with different stakeholders including barangay officials regarding proper waste disposal and the promotion of composting per household to help decrease solid wastes collected. WACS was not conducted due to pandemic restrictions.
observations.reason: WACS was still not conducted due to restrictions brought by the pandemic; the MSWMP review is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to: a. strictly adhere to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 for the timely submission of the GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report to COA; b. comply with the submission of the GPB and GAD AR to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO) and DILG Provincial Office for review and consolidation as provided under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Nos. 2016-01 and 2013-01; and c. submit immediately the Utilization Report for CY 2020 to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation of the expenses incurred.
observations.managementAction: The Management furnished the COA Office with copies of the GPB and GAD Accomplishment Report for CY 2021 and CY 2020 respectively. The GPB and GAD AR were submitted to the PPDO and DILG for review and consolidation. The GAD reports were furnished to COA with dates of submission noted; late submission was due to late assumption and designation of the GAD Focal Person and lack of awareness.
observations.reason: The GAD Focal Person’s late assumption and designation and lack of full awareness caused delay in submission; therefore not all requirements were met within the prescribed timeframe.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and other officials concerned to:
a. complete the processing requirements for the filing of request for authority to write-off for the receivables totaling P76,247.22 and submit to the Auditor for review and evaluation; 
b. set up Allowance for Impairment for recognition of possible losses from the receivables amounting to P76,247.22 and effect the adjustment in the amount of asset reflected in the Financial Statements for fair presentation; and 
c. continue to exert effort for its collectability when circumstances would warrant.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office planned to assess the collectability of the loans to be able to recognize the correct amounts of receivables for the recognition of the impairment loss and allowance for impairment. The Management planned to locate the borrowers and exert the effort in in the continuous tracing of the debtors.
observations.reason: The unfavorable circumstances brought by the CoVid-19 pandemic affected the assessment of the collectability of the loans to be able to recognize the correct amounts of receivables for the recognition of the impairment loss and allowance for impairment.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
a. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Service Officer to reconcile the
discrepancies noted between the RPCPPE and the Accounting records and effect the adjustment to
arrive at reconciled balances and to maintain updated PEC and PPELCs as basis for reconciliation and
validation.
b. The Municipal Accountant to reclassify the PPE totaling P1,506,571.43 to
appropriate PPE accounts according to its classification, specification and nature.
c. The Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop the small
value property items from the books by debiting Prior Years’ Adjustment of P481,550.52 and Accumulated
Depreciation of P886,977.61 and by crediting the respective PPE accounts of P1,368,528.13.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. draw the necessary adjustments to come up with the correct balance of the
Due to LGUs account and the related accounts affected; and
b. henceforth, strictly observe the proper classification of accounts as stated
under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the documents needed for recording and
recognition of the exchange transactions involving the award and sale of lots to
employees under the CSAP of the Municipality. Also draw Journal Entry
Voucher to set up the receivables, adjust the Land account and recognize the
revenue for the partial payment received/collected, to present fairly the Financial
Statements.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances
between their records pertaining to Cash in Bank-Local Currency Current
Account and immediately effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct
account’s balance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
coordinate with the Provincial Accountant and reconcile the balance of Due from
LGU and Due to LGU appearing in the books of the Municipality against the
balances of their respective reciprocal accounts appearing in the records of the
Provincial Government.

We also recommend that the necessary adjustment be made upon reconciliation
to correct the discrepancy to arrive at an accurate and correct balance of the
account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
review all the accounting entries that affect both reciprocal accounts, determine
the causes of discrepancy between their balances, and make the necessary
adjustment to reconcile the balance of the reciprocal accounts to ensure
reliability of the reflected balance in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
draw Journal Entry Vouchers to adjust the account in the General Fund and Trust
Fund books and reflect the correct balance of Trust Liabilities – DRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant in
coordination with other concerned officials to initiate action in cleaning the
dormant receivables in the books of accounts in accordance with COA Circular
No. 2016-005 through the following:
a. Exert more effort in enforcing the settlement of subject accounts receivables,
including that of the unremitted collections of former Assistant Municipal
Treasurer; and
b. Consider requesting for authority to write-off dormant receivables if
collectability is established to be nil, subject to the conditions set forth by the
above circular and submit to the Audit Team for review and evaluation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the immediate submission of the 18 DVs and
their supporting documents. Henceforth, require submission of accounts within
the period prescribed under Section 100 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No.
2009-006. The remedies provided under Section 122 of PD No. 1445 may be
availed of in instances of unjustified failure to submit said accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC Secretariat to
comply and adhere to the rules and regulations set forth in RA No. 9184 or the
Government Procurement Reform Act and to its Revised IRR as regards the
submission of required documentation in the procurement of Infrastructure
Projects so as to promote transparency, accountability, equity, efficiency, and
economy in the procurement process.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor Instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant:
i. To record purchases of office supplies, accountable forms, drugs and
medicines, medical, dental and laboratory supplies, food supplies, and
other supplies as debit to appropriate asset account using the perpetual
inventory system regardless of whether they were consumed within the
accounting period;
ii. To maintain the subsidiary ledger cards for each supplies and materials; and
iii. To record the issuances base on the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued
(RSMI).
b. Municipal General Services Officer:
i. To maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies;
ii. To prepare and submit monthly the RSMI to the Municipal Accountant for the
recording of issuances; and
iii. To prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories
(RPCI) by type of Inventory as of a given date.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO to cause the disposal
of property reported as unserviceable in the IIRUP in accordance with Section 79 of
PD No. 1445 and Section 170 to 180 of the Manual on the Rules and Procedures on
the Management of Property for Local Government Units to decongest the area
where these properties were stockpiled and recognize possible income from the
disposal thereof. Upon disposal, direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare Journal
Entry Voucher in order to derecognize these properties and adjust the overstatement
of PPE account in the books as required under IPSAS No. 17.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Officer to
submit copies of Contracts, POs and its supporting documents to the Office of
the Auditor within five working days after its perfection/issuance in compliance
with COA Circular No. 2009-001. Likewise, furnish the Auditor with a copy of
delivery documents as required under COA Circular No. 2009-002 to facilitate the
timely conduct of inspection of the deliveries.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to release the barangays’
shares from RPTI within five days after the end of each quarter as required under
Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
ensure that disbursement vouchers are processed and paid with complete
documentation to establish the propriety of expenses paid in compliance Section
9.1.3.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and submit the lacking documents listed in
the Appendices to comply with the documentary requirements for the specified
transactions.

We recommended that the Municipality provide specific budget equivalent to one
percent (1%) of IRA for LCPC and cause the maximum utilization of the allocated
fund to identified PPAs provided in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120
dated July 4, 2012, to ensure full strengthening and implementation of the LCPC.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the members of the LCPC
to prepare Local Development Plan and Local Investment Plan for Children and
inform the Sangguniang Bayan Members on the programs, projects and activities
(PPAs) that can be funded by the one percent IRA geared towards the achievement
of the objective on the promotion of rights and welfare of the children in the
community.

observations.managementAction:
Inventory Office submitted a copy of Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) received by the Office of the Auditor on April 13, 2021.
Reconciled records of Accounting against Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) of the GSO. 
Verified the various on each account.
Identified the proper PPE account of the various PPE and reclassify them.
Reassessed the PPEs with value of P10,000.00 and reclassify them.
observations.reason: The reconciliation was affected by the delay in the completion of physical inventory taking caused by the alternative work arrangement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances between their records pertaining to Cash in Bank-Local Currency Current Account and immediately effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct account’s balance.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation of accounts for CY 2019 has been performed to reconcile the balances between the Subsidiary Ledger and the Cashbooks.
observations.reason: The error in the computerized system of RPT administration, which was the reason the error on the computerized system was not immediately addressed. In addition, because of the alternative work arrangement in the Municipality of Pagsanjan, reconciliation of data took a little longer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to classify the outstanding balance of the long-term loans payable by presenting portion of loans due for settlement within twelve months as part of the current liabilities and the remaining amount which is due for settlement beyond one year as non-current liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: The current and non-current portions of the outstanding long-term loans were presented separately in the submitted CY2020 Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO Property Officer to prepare the Inventory List of Procured/Donated Items for DRRM and submit the same to the Audit Team for monitoring in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-02.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality submitted a list of the DRRM Equipment through its General Services Office, which was included in the RPCPPE forwarded to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The lack of manpower and lack of awareness regarding the submission of this separate report, caused the inability to fully implement the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to review all the accounting entries that affect both reciprocal accounts, determine the causes of discrepancy between their balances, and make the necessary adjustment to reconcile the balance of the reciprocal accounts to ensure reliability of the reflected balance in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management continually monitored the transactions involving the Due to and Due from reciprocal accounts.
observations.reason: The discrepancy amounting to P725,786.39 between the Due to the Intra-Agency Reciprocal accounts was caused by the payment of interest, which was deducted from the cash account of Special Education Fund (SEF) deposited in the Philippine National Bank (PNB), and reimbursed by the cash account under the General Fund (GF).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to release the barangays’ shares from RPTI within five days after the end of each quarter as required under Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer explained that there was system error encountered in the computerized system of Real Property Tax collection which caused the discrepancy in the generated reports from the system against the manual reconciliation of the receipts done by the Accounting Office. Consequently, there was a delay in the quarterly release of barangay shares from RPT collection. The Management has elevated the technical concerns with the service provided of the automated system which they took time to fix. The Municipal Treasurer also explained that the generation of the report and the release of shares were affected by the declaration of Enhanced Community Quarantine (ECQ) brought by the Covid-19 pandemic, that resulted in the disruption of work in the government. Thereafter, alternative work schedule thru work-from-home policy was implemented, thereby resulting in slow down in the generation of required reports. It was also mentioned that the voluminous transactions in the RPT collection during January of each year also contributed to the delays. Despite the given situation, the Management committed to comply with the prescribed and timely release of Barangay RPT shares in the succeeding quarters.
observations.reason: Due to the quarantine protocols caused by the CoVid-19 pandemic, reconciliation of data took a little longer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor undertake the immediate disposal of unserviceable property pursuant to the specified provisions of PD No. 1445, RA No. 7160 and in accordance with the procedures cited in COA Circular No. 89-296.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office has already started the process of identifying the unserviceable properties of the Municipality. A proposal to procure a pre-fabricated warehouse to store the unserviceable properties until disposal has been made.
observations.reason: The unavailability of a warehouse to shelter the identified unserviceable properties grouped according to classification, and the situation during the CoVid-19 outbreak hindered the full implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Development Council to continue its commitment to maximize the implementation of the development projects under the 20 percent Development Fund and enjoin the immediate utilization and implementation of the remaining unimplemented projects in order to fully achieve the 100 percent realization and completion of development projects.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer explained that the delay in the construction of slaughterhouse was due to the need to secure an Environmental Compliance Certificate (ECC) issuance from the Department of Environment and Natural Resources – Environmental Management Bureau (DENR-EMB), which the Municipality got only on September 2020. It also affected the payments for the project, where zero payment equates to zero utilization.
observations.reason: The delay in the construction of slaughterhouse was due to the need to secure first an ECC from DENR-EMB, which was granted only on September of CY 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. the Municipal Planning Coordinator and the Municipal Budget Officer to refrain from preparing programs or activities for the 20 percent DF which do not partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures; and
b. the Municipal Accountant to stop charging wages of job order personnel to the 20 percent DF.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant refrained from charging the wages to the 20 per cent DF. The Municipal Budget Officer issued a Supplemental Budget to cover the wages of the Job Order personnel for FY 2020.
observations.reason: The P179,693.94 was the total payment from January to August 2020, before the Auditor discussed the matter during the CY 2019 Exit Conference. The Municipal Budget Officer added that they have issued a Supplemental Budget to cover the wages of Job Order personnel for CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to continue its commitment to maximize the implementation of the disaster risk reduction programs, and further enhance the Municipality's disaster preparedness activities so that maximum benefits may be derived by the constituents. We also enjoined the utilization of P1,787,748.33 from prior years and P495,569.57 from CY 2019, subject to existing rules and regulations to further enhance the disaster preparedness activities so that maximum benefits may be derived by the constituents.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO continued its commitment to maximize the implementation of disaster risk reduction programs, as evidenced by the 88.33 per cent utilization rate of the Mitigation Fund for CY 2020. The Municipality furnished Appropriation Ordinance No. 05-2020 dated September 17, 2020 where the unutilized LDRRMF balances for CYs 2012 to 2019 and the CY 2020 appropriation for MF were realigned to be used for the Municipality's Supplemental Budget for FY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require both the LDRRM Officer and the Municipal Accountant to prepare the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund, and submit the same to the COA, duly certified by the Municipal Accountant, pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting and MDRRM Offices continually monitored and prepared the monthly reports. The MDRRMO submitted the reports to national agencies as a requirement for the Gawad Kalasag but failed to submit copies of the same to COA. The Municipal Accountant committed to coordinate with the assigned personnel in the MDRRM Office to comply with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: The reports were prepared and submitted to oversight offices; however, copies were not furnished to COA as required.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to:
a. strictly adhere to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 for the timely submission of the GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report to the COA; 
b. comply with the submission of the GPB and GAD AR to the Provincial Planning and Development Office and the DILG Provincial Office for their review, endorsement and consolidation as provided under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Nos. 2016-01 and 2013-01; and 
c. submit immediately the Utilization Report for CY 2019 to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation of the expenses incurred.
observations.managementAction: The Management provided the Audit Team with a copy of the GPB and GAD AR. The reports were claimed to have been submitted to the Provincial Office; however, the copies provided to the Audit Team do not bear the date of receipt by said offices. No management action has been undertaken to submit the Utilization Report for CY 2019 to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: The submission was affected by the delay in submission of the reports to the Provincial Office, which usually takes place during January to March. The copies provided to the Audit Team do not bear the date of receipt by the Provincial Planning and Development Office nor the Provincial GAD Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MSWMB and the MENRO to review and update the Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality every two years or as the need arises, in compliance with Section 4 (g), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The MSWMB planned to coordinate with the Provincial Office for the conduct of the Waste Analysis and Characterization Study (WACS) and consultation with the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC) for possible updates. The MSWMP updating requires WACS which was not conducted due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
observations.reason: The delay in conducting WACS and the COVID-19 pandemic hindered the conduct of the Waste Analysis and Characterization Study and the updates.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reorganize the MSWMB and conform with the membership requirements in accordance with Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The MSWMB was reorganized by virtue of the following Executive Orders: 1. EO No. 12, Series of 2018 dated August 2, 2019 - effective for 3rd and 4th quarters of CY 2018 until the 2nd quarter of CY 2019; 2. EO No. 06, Series of 2019 dated August 8, 2018 - effective from the 3rd quarter of CY 2019 until the 2nd quarter of CY 2020; and 3. EO No. 104, Series of 2020 dated August 26, 2020, covering 3rd quarter of CY 2020 to 2nd quarter of CY 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO and the Inventory
Committee to conduct complete inventory taking of all property.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO and the Municipal
Accountant:
 to conduct regular reconciliation of their records in order to further decrease the
discrepancy and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted;
 to prioritize the tracing and verification of the unidentified beginning balances of
P6,815,407.41 in the inventory report;
 to identify and reclassify property to its proper PPE account; and
 reclassify those PPEs with a value of less than P10,000.00 and with a life
expectancy of more than one year as Inventories and issue Inventory Custodian
Slip upon issuance for control and monitoring purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances between their records pertaining to
Cash in Bank-Local Currency Current Account and immediately effect the
necessary adjustments to reflect the correct account’s balance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
classify the outstanding balance of the long-term loans payable by presenting the
portion of loans due for settlement within twelve months as part of the current
liabilities and the remaining amount which is due for settlement beyond one year
as non-current liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO Property Officer to
prepare the Inventory List of Procured/Donated Items for DRRM and submit the
same to the Audit Team for monitoring, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-
02.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
other officials concerned to:
a. complete the processing of requirements for the filing of request for authority
to write-off for the receivables totaling P76,247.22 and submit to the Auditor for
review and evaluation;
b. set up Allowance for Impairment for recognition of possible losses from the
receivables amounting to P76,247.22 and effect the adjustment in the amount of
asset reflected in the Financial Statements for fair presentation; and
c. continue to exert effort for its collectability when circumstances would warrant.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
review all the accounting entries that affect both reciprocal accounts, determine the
causes of discrepancy between their balances, and make the necessary
adjustments to reconcile the balances of the reciprocal accounts to ensure
reliability of the reflected balance in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the
Municipal Accountant to release the barangays’ shares from RPTI within five days
after the end of each quarter as required under Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor undertake the immediate disposal of
unserviceable property pursuant to the specified provisions of PD No. 1445, RA No.
7160 and in accordance with the procedures cited in COA Circular No. 89-296.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Development Council
to continue its commitment to maximize the implementation of the development
projects under the 20 percent Development Fund and enjoin the immediate
utilization and implementation of the remaining unimplemented projects in order to
fully achieve the 100 percent realization and completion of development projects.

a. the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Budget
Officer to refrain from preparing programs or activities for the 20 percent DF
which do not partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures; and
b. the Municipal Accountant to stop charging wages of job order personnel to the
20 percent DF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to continue its commitment to
maximize the implementation of the disaster risk reduction and management
programs to upgrade the Municipality’s disaster resiliency and preparedness.
We also enjoined the utilization of the P1,787,748.33 from prior years and
P495,569.57 from CY 2019, subject to existing rules and regulations to further

derived by the constituents.
